title: The Law of God - WCF Chapter 19
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Law of God - WCF Chapter 19 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt
outline: Paragraph 1: God gave the moral law to Adam as a covenant of works, binding on all humanity
Paragraph 2: After the fall, the moral law continued as a rule of righteousness and was codified in the Ten Commandments
Paragraph 3: God gave ceremonial laws to Israel as types and shadows pointing to Christ, now abrogated under the New Testament
Paragraph 4: God gave judicial/civil laws to Israel as a body politic, expired with that state, binding only in general equity
Paragraphs 5-7: The moral law remains binding on believers not for justification but for sanctification and obedience
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro works through Westminster Confession of Faith Chapter 19, 'The Law of God,' tracing the moral law from creation through the fall to the New Testament. He explains the threefold division of Old Testament law—moral, ceremonial, and civil—showing that the moral law (Ten Commandments) remains binding on all people, the ceremonial laws were fulfilled and abrogated by Christ, and the civil laws of Israel expired with the Israelite state. The lesson includes discussion of how the Ten Commandments were implicit in the garden of Eden and remain the standard for Christian sanctification today.
transcript:
## Introduction: Why the Law of God Matters

A proper understanding of the law of God is essential in order to glorify God. You cannot truly glorify God apart from understanding what he says concerning himself and what he requires of us. And something we do not often consider: a proper understanding of the law of God is also essential for a proper understanding of the gospel. There is no gospel without the law, and there is no proper understanding of the gospel without the law.

Today we are walking through Chapter 19 of the Westminster Confession of Faith, "Of the Law of God."

## Paragraph 1: The Law Before the Fall

> *"God gave to Adam a law as a covenant of works, by which he bound him and all his posterity to personal, entire, exact, and perpetual obedience; promised life upon the fulfilling and threatened death upon the breach of it; and endued him with power and ability to keep it."*

Before the fall, God gave Adam a law — and Adam was able to obey it perfectly. He was not created neutral, capable of going either way. He was created *righteous*, with inherent righteousness we do not possess. He was able to sin, but also able not to sin, and inclined toward obedience.

Even before the Ten Commandments were given at Sinai, the moral law was present in the garden and written on Adam's heart, as Romans 2 confirms. We can trace the commandments through the early chapters of Genesis:

- **Third commandment** — Genesis 1:28: Adam and Eve are told to have dominion over God's creation, which is one way God reveals his name.
- **Fifth and Seventh commandments** — Genesis 1:28: The command to form a family (husband, wife, children).
- **Fourth commandment** — Genesis 2:3: God establishes the Sabbath as a creation ordinance, binding as long as creation endures.
- **First and Second commandments** — Genesis 3:8: Fellowship with God regulated by the commands to have no other gods and to make no images.
- **Ninth commandment** — Genesis 3:1: The introduction of lying and truth.
- **Eighth commandment** — Genesis 2:15: God gives the garden to Adam and Eve — do not take what belongs to another.

The covenant in paragraph 1 has all the elements of a biblical covenant: the parties (God and Adam as representative of all humanity), the condition (obedience), the promise (life), and the penalty (death).

This law was binding on Adam "and all his posterity." That includes every human being, believer and unbeliever alike. Every human being, as a descendant of Adam, is under obligation to obey the moral law of God.

## Paragraph 2: The Law After the Fall and the Ten Commandments

> *"This law, after his fall, continued to be a perfect rule of righteousness, and, as such, was delivered by God upon Mount Sinai, in ten commandments."*

The law did not disappear after the fall. It was codified in written form at Sinai in what Hebrew calls the "ten words" — the Decalogue — divided into two tablets: the first four commandments governing our duty toward God, and the remaining six governing our duty toward one another. Jesus summarized these as love God with all your heart, soul, mind, and strength, and love your neighbor as yourself.

For those who are in Christ, the law does not justify — Christ's obedience to the law justifies us. We are "not under law but under grace" (Romans 6:14) in the sense that we are not justified by legal obedience. But Paul immediately goes on to insist that believers walk in obedience (Romans 6:15–17). The moral law remains binding on the believer for sanctification — not as the means of justification, but as the standard of Godly living.

Note also the division between Roman Catholic/Lutheran numbering and the Reformed numbering: they combine the first two commandments and split the tenth. The Reformed tradition counts each commandment as the Bible presents them.

## Paragraph 3: The Ceremonial Law

> *"Beside this law, commonly called moral, God was pleased to give to the people of Israel, as a church under age, ceremonial laws, containing several typical ordinances, partly of worship, prefiguring Christ, his graces, actions, sufferings, and benefits; and partly holding forth divers instructions of moral duties. All which ceremonial laws are now abrogated under the New Testament."*

To help Israel understand the moral law and to prefigure the coming Messiah, God gave ceremonial laws — sacrifices, dietary regulations, purity laws, feast days, and all the ordinances of the tabernacle and temple. The book of Hebrews calls these "shadows of things to come":

> *"So let no one judge you in food or in drink, or regarding a festival or a new moon or sabbaths, which are a shadow of things to come, but the substance is of Christ."* (Colossians 2:16–17)

Once the reality has come, the shadow is no longer needed. Christ has fulfilled all these ceremonial laws. The sacrifices pointed to his sacrifice. The priestly system pointed to his priesthood. The dietary laws signified the holiness of God's people looking toward the Messiah — a holiness we now possess *in Christ*. We are therefore not bound to observe mixed-fabric laws, dietary restrictions, or annual feasts. Those served their purpose and are now abrogated.

When someone says, "You say you obey the Bible, but I don't see you wearing a single fabric," the answer is straightforward: that person has misunderstood what the Bible teaches. The ceremonial law is fulfilled in Christ.

## Paragraph 4: The Civil Law

> *"To them also, as a body politic, he gave sundry judicial laws, which expired together with the state of that people; not obliging any other now, further than the general equity thereof may require."*

God also gave Israel civil laws governing how citizens should relate to one another. These expired with the state of Israel as a theocratic nation. The Confession is explicitly opposed to *theonomy* — the doctrine that the civil laws of the Old Testament, including their penalties, should be reinstated in modern civil society. Those laws are abrogated, though the general equity behind them may inform Christian thinking about justice.

The one civil law that persists across covenants is the death penalty for murder, because it is grounded not in the Mosaic covenant but in the Noahic covenant — rooted in the image of God that every human being bears (Genesis 9:6).

## Paragraphs 5–7: The Law and the Believer

The remaining paragraphs (which we leave for private reading) address how the moral law is not a means of justification for the believer but remains the rule for Christian obedience and sanctification. The law drives us to Christ by showing us our sin. It then guides us in the walk of obedience. Being "not under law but under grace" does not mean the law is irrelevant to the Christian life — it means we are no longer judged by our obedience to it, but by Christ's obedience for us.

A sound hermeneutical principle: unless God explicitly says in the New Testament that an Old Testament command no longer applies, we continue to follow it. The ceremonial laws are explicitly set aside. The moral law is not. We follow the moral law — not to be justified, but because that is how we grow in Godliness and discern God's will.
